# Transcrição: Should_You_Start_New_Campaigns_with_Creative_Testing_

- **Fonte:** Should_You_Start_New_Campaigns_with_Creative_Testing_
- **Duração:** 03:22
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:00]

Today's podcast question is about when to use creative testing. Let's go. Hi, John. This is Amanda with a podcast question regarding creative testing. I recently taken a pause from doing campaigns for my business. And so now I'm ready to launch our very first campaign and we have new creatives and wondering should I be launching this brand new campaign as setting it up as creative testing or should that come afterwards as I receive new creative? Hey, Amanda. Great question. So creative testing is a major focus of mine right now. I love talking about it. There isn't a true right or wrong here, but I certainly have a preferred approach and there's a reason behind that. Whenever I'm ready to publish new ads, I start them with a test using the creative testing tool. That doesn't mean a separate campaign or ad set for testing either. I create the test in the active ad set where the ads will live when the test is over. Now, your question is about starting a new campaign and ad set. And there's a slight caveat here. So normally when you initiate a test using the creative testing tool, a percentage of your campaign or ad set budget will be set aside for the test. Meta typically recommends around 20% of that budget for testing. But if it's a new campaign, you'd obviously want to use 100%. Now, in theory, Meta should use more than 20% if the only ads in the campaign are those in your test. I just go ahead and manually choose the full budget when setting up your test. Now, there are two reasons why I do this at the beginning when starting new ads. The first is that it helps answer questions I know I'll have later. I get to see how individual ads perform when Meta gives them equal budget. I don't have to wonder weeks down the road what would happen if an ad that's not getting any impressions received some budget. I'd have that data from the initial test. The second reason I start new ads this way is due to an annoying quirk with creative testing. You cannot test existing ads with a creative testing tool. And because of that, you're stuck with a very annoying scenario if you want to test an active ad weeks after it started running. You'd need to duplicate those existing ads and recreate them to run the test. And those test ads would then run while their duplicates also run. It's just super messy and you can avoid all of that by starting new ads with a test. As a side note, consider the number of ads, the length of the test, and your budget assigned to the test as well. You want to get meaningful data from that test. If you're optimizing for a purchase and running a creative test of five ads that split up $50 per day for a week, you're not going to get meaningful data. You're spending $10 per day for each ad. That's where you might want to test two ads at a time or extend the test to two weeks. Hope this helps.

## [03:00 - 03:22]

Thanks for your question, Amanda. You want your question to be answered on a future episode, send it my way. Go to johnlumor.com slash question and record your question today. You may just be featured on a future podcast. Otherwise, thanks for joining me again today. Until next time, do awesome things. I'm out.
